5: The closing oracle of Malachi (Malachi 4:5-6) is also the closing oracle of the OT: 'Behold, I will send you Elijah the prophet before the great and awesome day of the LORD comes. And he will turn the hearts of fathers to their children and hearts of children to their fathers, lest I come and strike the land with a decree of utter destruction.' The Hebrew canon ends here — with a promise of Elijah's return and a warning of cursing if he is rejected. The NT opens with John the Baptist filling this role (Luke 1:17; Matthew 11:14; Malachi 17:10-13). The OT ends in expectation; the NT opens in fulfillment. The 400-year inter-testamental silence is the space between Malachi's promise and Matthew's fulfillment — the waiting for the forerunner who will announce the Lord's coming.
1: "For look, the day is coming that will burn like a furnace (tanur), and all the arrogant (zedim) and every evildoer will be stubble (qash). The coming day will burn them up, says YHWH of Hosts, so that it will leave them neither root nor branch." Yom ba (the day is coming) — the eschatological day of YHWH, announced throughout the prophets. Tanur (furnace) — the smelting oven; a more intense image than the refiner's fire of Malachi 3:2. Qash (stubble) — the driest, most combustible material; what survives harvest after the grain is taken. The total destruction — "neither root nor branch" (lo ye'ezov lahem shoresh va-anaph) — eliminates both origin and future, a comprehensive end to the arrogant's dynasty.
2: "But for you who fear my name, the Sun of Righteousness (shemesh tsedakah) will rise with healing in its wings (marpe' bikhnafeha). You will go out and leap about like calves from the stall." Shemesh tsedakah — the great Messianic title that has generated enormous interpretive discussion. Tsedakah = righteousness/vindication; the sun as the source of life and light paired with the quality of divine righteousness. The image may be of YHWH himself dawning as a sun (Psalms 84:11, "YHWH God is a sun and shield") or of a messianic figure. Marpe' bikhnafeha — "healing in its wings" — the word kanaph (wing) evokes the winged solar disk of ancient Near Eastern iconography as well as YHWH's wings of refuge (Psalms 91:4; Ruth 2:12; Matthew 23:37). The calves from the stall imagery is a picture of the joy that replaces the mourning of Malachi 3:14.
3: "You will trample the wicked, who will be ashes under the soles of your feet on the day when I act, says YHWH of Hosts." The reversal is complete: the arrogant who appeared to prosper (Malachi 3:15) will be ashes under the feet of those who feared YHWH's name. The "day when I act" (yom asher ani oseh) is the same eschatological day announced in Malachi 4:1. The righteous who walk on the ashes of the wicked is a strong image of complete covenantal reversal — but the context makes clear that this is YHWH's action (ani oseh — I am acting), not human vengeance.
4: "Remember the Torah of my servant Moses (torat Mosheh avdi), the statutes and ordinances I commanded him at Horeb for all Israel." Before the great eschatological arrival, the community is anchored to what they already have. Torat Mosheh avdi — "Torah of Moses my servant" — the foundational covenant document. Horeb (= Sinai) is the Deuteronomic name for the mountain of the covenant. The command to remember (zikru) is the covenant-keeping imperative: the Torah already given is sufficient guide until the day of the messengers. The OT closes (at least in the Hebrew canon as ordered in the Talmud) with the community pointed simultaneously backward to Moses and forward to Elijah.
6: "He will turn the hearts of fathers to their children and the hearts of children to their fathers, or else I will come and strike the land with a decree of destruction (cherem)." The social fracture announced in Micah 7:6 — household division — is what Elijah's ministry will heal. Hesheev lev avot al-banim ve-lev banim al-avotam — the restored intergenerational covenant, the household as the site of covenant transmission, healed by the Elijah-forerunner's ministry. The alternative — cherem — is the most severe covenant sanction: the total destruction applied to Canaan (Joshua 6:17-18) and threatened against Israel in Deuteronomy 13:16. The OT closes with this hinge: either the ministry of Elijah-return and the turning of hearts, or the cherem. The NT announces that the first has happened in John the Baptist, opening the way for the second era.
